dispatch.return_message: All three rounds failed because BioPsyKit's `_resample_index` hard-codes `pd.to_datetime(raw_integers, unit='us')` but the dataset's DatetimeIndex stores epoch values as nanoseconds (~1.57e18), so the reinterpretation as microseconds overflows pandas' datetime range — and no amount of timezone manipulation (rounds 2 and 3's tactical patches) changes the underlying nanosecond integer magnitudes that cause the crash.

prompt.content:
You are a research-failure curator producing a **deep negative-knowledge
record** that summarises a 3-round Self-Debug exploration of one
ScienceAgentBench task. All three rounds failed.

The 3-round exploration was a strong baseline: at round 2 the agent saw
its round-1 code + full stderr + eval output (covering memory); at round
3 it saw the round-2 code + full stderr + eval output. Despite this, all
three attempts failed. Your NK record should reflect that this is *not*
a trivial one-shot failure — three different concrete approaches have
been ruled out.

# Task

Task id: `044`

Task instruction:
> Analyze the inertial measurement unit (IMU) data collected during sleep and compute sleep endpoints. Load the given data and compute the following sleep endpoints: time of falling asleep, time of awakening, and total duration spent sleeping. The three values should be saved in a JSON file "pred_results/imu_pred.json", and the keys for them are "sleep_onset", "wake_onset", and "total_sleep_duration", respectively.

# Inputs you may read (and only these)

You will read up to 11 files. Do not read anything else.

**Round 1 (no memory, free attempt):**
1. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round1/candidate.py` — round-1 script
2. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round1/exec.log` — stdout / stderr / runtime errors
3. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round1/eval.log` — evaluator output IF it ran
4. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round1/reasoning.md` — round-1 agent's reasoning

**Round 2 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 1):**
5. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/candidate.py`
6. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/exec.log`
7. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 2: ABSENT — script crashed before producing output, evaluator not run.
8. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/reasoning.md`

**Round 3 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 2):**
9. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/candidate.py`
10. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/exec.log`
11. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 3: ABSENT — script crashed before producing output, evaluator not run.
12. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_044/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/reasoning.md`

Read all available files before writing.

# Your output (exactly one Write call)

Write a JSON file via the Write tool to:

`/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_044_deep.json`

The schema extends the base NK with two fields specific to deep curation:

```json
{
  "task_id": "044",
  "depth": 3,
  "rounds_summary": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "attempted_route": "<= 200 chars; what method/library/parameter was tried",
      "observation":     "<= 200 chars; specific failure signature"
    },
    {"round": 2, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."},
    {"round": 3, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."}
  ],
  "ruled_out_routes": [
    "<= 150 chars per item; specific (library + parameter) combinations that have been empirically shown not to work on this task. List 2-4 items.",
    "..."
  ],
  "synthesised_diagnosis": "<= 400 chars; ONE coherent mechanism-level explanation that accounts for ALL THREE failures. Not a per-round list; a unified diagnosis. This is the load-bearing claim of the deep NK — make it specific.",
  "failure": {
    "layer": "implementation_failure | communication_failure | method_failure",
    "scope": "local_failure | regime_bound_failure | general_failure",
    "degree": "contradicted | partial | inconclusive | unstable | artifact_driven | overclaimed",
    "recommended_action": "retry | change_method | narrow_claim | abandon_route",
    "risk": "low_risk_omission | medium_risk_drift | high_risk_false_progress"
  },
  "rationale": "<= 300 chars; one mechanism-level claim explaining WHY the synthesised diagnosis is correct.",
  "recommended_alternative": "<= 400 chars; ONE specific concrete fix the next attempt should try. Must NOT be a method already in ruled_out_routes. Must name specific APIs/library functions/parameters."
}
```

# Hard rules

1. Use Read on any subset of the listed input files. Do NOT read other files.
2. Use Write tool EXACTLY ONCE, to `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_044_deep.json`, with the JSON object.
3. Do NOT use Bash, Edit, Grep, Glob, or any other tool.
4. Do NOT wrap in markdown code fences. The file must be parseable by `json.load`.
5. The `recommended_alternative` must NOT recommend anything that already
   appears in `ruled_out_routes`. The whole point of deep NK is that the
   next attempt avoids what's already known to fail.
6. The `failure.*` taxonomy values are fixed; do not invent new values.
7. After the single Write, respond with ONE short sentence describing the
   synthesised diagnosis that ties all three round failures together.

# Guidance

The `synthesised_diagnosis` field is the highest-value piece of a deep NK.
It is the difference between "three independent failure snapshots" and
"one coherent understanding of why this task is hard". Write it as if you
were explaining to a colleague: *"Across all three rounds, the agent kept
running into <mechanism>; it can't be fixed by <tactical patches the
agents tried> because <reason>."* The `recommended_alternative` should
then follow from this diagnosis, not from any single round's local error.

If the three rounds revealed unrelated bugs (i.e., each round fixed the
prior round's bug but exposed a new one), say so — that's also a
legitimate synthesised diagnosis ("the task has at least three
independent gotchas, of which we've now uncovered all three"), and the
`recommended_alternative` should bundle fixes for all three.

r1_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

The task requires computing three sleep endpoints from IMU accelerometer data: sleep onset time, wake onset time, and total sleep duration. The domain knowledge specifies using `biopsykit.sleep.sleep_processing_pipeline.predict_pipeline_acceleration()` from the BioPsyKit library.

Steps:
1. Load `sleep_data.pkl` using pickle (the preview shows a DataFrame with time index and IMU columns).
2. Set the `time` column as a DatetimeIndex if not already.
3. Extract the three acceleration columns (`acc_x`, `acc_y`, `acc_z`) and rename them to the short form (`x`, `y`, `z`) that BioPsyKit expects.
4. Pass the acceleration DataFrame to `predict_pipeline_acceleration()`.
5. Extract the three endpoints from the returned result object/dict, handling both attribute and dict-key access patterns.
6. Convert timestamps to ISO strings and duration to numeric minutes.
7. Write the result to `pred_results/imu_pred.json` with the required keys.

## Library choices

- **biopsykit**: Mandated by domain knowledge; provides the `predict_pipeline_acceleration` function that encapsulates the full sleep-processing pipeline (activity index computation, rest-period detection, endpoint extraction).
- **pandas**: Used for data loading and DatetimeIndex manipulation, which BioPsyKit expects.
- **pickle**: The dataset is a `.pkl` file, so standard pickle load is the straightforward approach.
- **json / os**: Standard library for output serialization and directory creation.

## Risks

- **Column naming**: BioPsyKit's pipeline may expect acceleration columns named `x`, `y`, `z` rather than `acc_x`, `acc_y`, `acc_z`. The script strips the `acc_` prefix before calling the pipeline.
- **Return type uncertainty**: The return type of `predict_pipeline_acceleration` is not directly inspectable without running code. The script defensively handles both dict and object (attribute-access) return shapes, and also handles timedelta vs. numeric duration.
- **Timezone handling**: The data preview shows timezone-aware timestamps (`+02:00`). `pd.to_datetime` preserves this; BioPsyKit typically handles tz-aware indices correctly.
- **Output format**: `total_sleep_duration` is converted to minutes (float) for a consistent numeric JSON value, while onset times are serialized as ISO 8601 strings.

## Use of memory

No prior memory was available (first attempt). No external files were read beyond the prompt itself. All decisions are based on the BioPsyKit documentation implied by the domain knowledge hint and the data preview in the prompt.

r2_candidate.content:
import os
import json
import pickle
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import biopsykit as bp
from biopsykit.sleep.sleep_processing_pipeline import predict_pipeline_acceleration

# Paths
data_path = "benchmark/datasets/sleep_imu_data/sleep_data.pkl"
output_dir = "pred_results"
output_path = os.path.join(output_dir, "imu_pred.json")

# Load data
with open(data_path, "rb") as f:
    data = pickle.load(f)

# Ensure it's a DataFrame
if not isinstance(data, pd.DataFrame):
    data = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Set 'time' as the index if it is a column
if "time" in data.columns:
    data = data.set_index("time")

# Ensure the index is a proper DatetimeIndex
data.index = pd.to_datetime(data.index)

# Infer sampling rate in Hz from the median time difference between consecutive samples
time_diffs = pd.Series(data.index).diff().dropna()
median_diff_sec = time_diffs.median().total_seconds()
sampling_rate = round(1.0 / median_diff_sec)  # Hz, e.g. 200 Hz

# Extract acceleration columns (acc_x, acc_y, acc_z)
acc_cols = [c for c in data.columns if c.startswith("acc_")]
acc_data = data[acc_cols].copy()

# Run the BioPsyKit sleep processing pipeline
sleep_results = predict_pipeline_acceleration(acc_data, sampling_rate=sampling_rate)

# Helper to serialize timestamps and durations
def to_serializable(val):
    if val is None:
        return None
    if hasattr(val, "isoformat"):
        return val.isoformat()
    if hasattr(val, "total_seconds"):
        return val.total_seconds() / 60.0  # convert timedelta to minutes
    try:
        return float(val)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        return str(val)

# Extract the three required endpoints from the result
# sleep_results may be a dict or a namespace/object
def get_field(results, *keys):
    for key in keys:
        if isinstance(results, dict) and key in results:
            return results[key]
        if hasattr(results, key):
            return getattr(results, key)
    return None

sleep_onset_raw = get_field(sleep_results, "sleep_onset", "sleep_onset_time")
wake_onset_raw = get_field(sleep_results, "wake_onset", "wake_onset_time", "wake_time")
total_sleep_duration_raw = get_field(
    sleep_results, "total_sleep_duration", "sleep_duration", "duration"
)

sleep_onset = to_serializable(sleep_onset_raw)
wake_onset = to_serializable(wake_onset_raw)
total_sleep_duration = to_serializable(total_sleep_duration_raw)

# Build and save output
output = {
    "sleep_onset": sleep_onset,
    "wake_onset": wake_onset,
    "total_sleep_duration": total_sleep_duration,
}

os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump(output, f, indent=2, default=str)

print(f"Saved results to {output_path}")
print(json.dumps(output, indent=2, default=str))

r2_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

Round 1 failed with `TypeError: predict_pipeline_acceleration() missing 1 required positional argument: 'sampling_rate'`. The fix is straightforward: infer the sampling rate from the data and pass it as a keyword argument.

Steps:
1. Load `sleep_data.pkl` with pickle; if the loaded object has a `time` column, set it as the DatetimeIndex.
2. Infer sampling rate by computing the median inter-sample interval from consecutive timestamps and taking its reciprocal (rounded to the nearest integer Hz). For IMU data collected at ~200 Hz the median gap is ~5 ms, giving `sampling_rate = 200`.
3. Slice out the three acceleration columns (`acc_x`, `acc_y`, `acc_z`) and pass the DataFrame together with `sampling_rate` to `predict_pipeline_acceleration()`.
4. Extract `sleep_onset`, `wake_onset`, and `total_sleep_duration` from the returned object (which may be a dict or a namespace). Serialize timestamps via `.isoformat()` and timedelta durations as total minutes.
5. Write the three values to `pred_results/imu_pred.json`.

## Library choices

- **biopsykit** — mandated by the task domain knowledge; `sleep_processing_pipeline.predict_pipeline_acceleration` is the prescribed entry point.
- **pandas** — data loading and datetime handling; index inference via `Series.diff()` and `median()`.
- **json / pickle** — standard-library IO; no extra dependencies needed.

## Risks

- **Unknown return type**: BioPsyKit may return different result structures across versions. The `get_field` helper tries both dict-key and attribute access, and also checks several plausible key names (`sleep_onset`, `wake_onset`, `total_sleep_duration` / `sleep_duration` / `duration`).
- **Sampling rate edge cases**: if the index contains duplicate timestamps the median diff could be zero; rounding to the nearest integer also coerces non-integer rates. For a standard IMU file this is unlikely to matter.
- **Timezone handling**: the preview shows timezone-aware timestamps (`+02:00`). `pd.to_datetime` preserves this automatically; `isoformat()` re-encodes the offset in the output string.

## Use of memory

Round 1's `exec.log` revealed the exact `TypeError`. No eval.log existed, so there was no scorer feedback to incorporate. The only change needed was adding `sampling_rate=sampling_rate` to the `predict_pipeline_acceleration` call and computing that value from the index.

r3_candidate.content:
import os
import json
import pickle
import pandas as pd
from biopsykit.sleep.sleep_processing_pipeline import predict_pipeline_acceleration

# ── Paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
data_path  = "benchmark/datasets/sleep_imu_data/sleep_data.pkl"
output_dir = "pred_results"
output_path = os.path.join(output_dir, "imu_pred.json")

# ── Load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
with open(data_path, "rb") as f:
    data = pickle.load(f)

if not isinstance(data, pd.DataFrame):
    data = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Promote 'time' column to index if needed
if "time" in data.columns:
    data = data.set_index("time")

# Ensure DatetimeIndex
data.index = pd.to_datetime(data.index)

# ── Fix: strip timezone ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# BioPsyKit's _resample_index calls pd.to_datetime(values, unit="us", utc=True).
# With a tz-aware index pandas produces nanosecond integers (.asi8 ~1.57e18),
# but the function treats them as microseconds → OutOfBoundsDatetime overflow.
# Converting to UTC then removing tz gives a naive UTC DatetimeIndex whose
# integer representation (ns) stays in the valid range for the library call.
if data.index.tz is not None:
    data.index = data.index.tz_convert("UTC").tz_localize(None)

# ── Sampling rate ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
time_diffs = pd.Series(data.index).diff().dropna()
median_diff_sec = time_diffs.median().total_seconds()
sampling_rate = round(1.0 / median_diff_sec)   # Hz

# ── Acceleration columns only ──────────────────────────────────────────────
acc_cols = [c for c in data.columns if c.startswith("acc_")]
acc_data = data[acc_cols].copy()

# ── Run BioPsyKit sleep pipeline ───────────────────────────────────────────
sleep_results = predict_pipeline_acceleration(acc_data, sampling_rate=sampling_rate)

# ── Extract the three endpoints ────────────────────────────────────────────
def get_field(results, *keys):
    for key in keys:
        if isinstance(results, dict) and key in results:
            return results[key]
        if hasattr(results, key):
            return getattr(results, key)
    return None

def to_serializable(val):
    if val is None:
        return None
    if hasattr(val, "isoformat"):
        return val.isoformat()
    if hasattr(val, "total_seconds"):
        return val.total_seconds() / 60.0   # timedelta → minutes
    try:
        return float(val)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        return str(val)

sleep_onset_raw         = get_field(sleep_results, "sleep_onset", "sleep_onset_time")
wake_onset_raw          = get_field(sleep_results, "wake_onset",  "wake_onset_time", "wake_time")
total_sleep_duration_raw = get_field(
    sleep_results, "total_sleep_duration", "sleep_duration", "duration"
)

output = {
    "sleep_onset":           to_serializable(sleep_onset_raw),
    "wake_onset":            to_serializable(wake_onset_raw),
    "total_sleep_duration":  to_serializable(total_sleep_duration_raw),
}

# ── Save ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump(output, f, indent=2, default=str)

print(f"Saved results to {output_path}")
print(json.dumps(output, indent=2, default=str))

r3_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

Round 2 crashed inside `biopsykit.signals.imu.rest_periods._resample_index` with:

```
OutOfBoundsDatetime: cannot convert input with unit 'us'
```

Root cause: `_resample_index` calls `pd.to_datetime(index_resample, unit="us", utc=True)`, where `index_resample` is the integer representation of the DatetimeIndex. When the index is timezone-aware, pandas represents it internally as nanoseconds since epoch (`.asi8` values ≈ 1.57 × 10¹⁸). The library then reinterprets those nanosecond integers as *microseconds*, which overflows pandas' supported range.

Fix: convert the timezone-aware index to UTC then strip the timezone (`tz_localize(None)`) before passing `acc_data` to `predict_pipeline_acceleration`. The resulting naive DatetimeIndex keeps the correct UTC wall-clock times while preventing the integer-scale mismatch inside BioPsyKit.

Everything else (sampling-rate inference, acceleration column selection, field extraction, JSON serialisation) was already correct in round 2 and is retained unchanged.

## Library choices

- **biopsykit** — mandated by the task's domain knowledge note; `predict_pipeline_acceleration` is the prescribed entry point.
- **pandas** — data loading, index manipulation, sampling-rate calculation.
- **pickle / json** — `.pkl` input, `.json` output as required.

## Risks

- BioPsyKit's result schema is not public API; field names are inferred from common patterns (`sleep_onset`, `wake_onset`, `total_sleep_duration`). The `get_field` helper tries multiple aliases so a minor naming difference won't silently yield `None`.
- `total_sleep_duration` may be a `timedelta`; the serialiser converts it to minutes (float). If the evaluator expects seconds or a string, this could differ — but minutes is the most natural human-readable unit for sleep duration.
- Sampling rate is inferred from median inter-sample interval; if data has gaps this may round incorrectly, but for a continuous overnight recording this is robust.

## Use of memory

Read `round2_B2/candidate.py` and `round2_B2/exec.log`. The exec log pinpointed the exact line (`pd.to_datetime(index_resample, unit="us", utc=True)`) and the overflow value (`1.567469178251953e+18`), making the nanosecond-vs-microsecond confusion unambiguous. No other files were read.

rounds_summary.observation: TypeError: predict_pipeline_acceleration() missing 1 required positional argument: 'sampling_rate'. Script crashed at line 34 in 1.8 s; no output produced.

rounds_summary.observation: OutOfBoundsDatetime inside biopsykit.signals.imu.rest_periods._resample_index: pd.to_datetime(values, unit='us') overflows on nanosecond .asi8 values (~1.57e18).

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Same as round 2 but strips timezone via tz_convert('UTC').tz_localize(None) before calling predict_pipeline_acceleration, hoping to lower .asi8 magnitudes
rounds_summary.observation: Identical OutOfBoundsDatetime overflow (same value 1.567469178251953e+18). tz_localize(None) does not change nanosecond .asi8 integers; crash recurs unchanged.

ruled_out_routes: predict_pipeline_acceleration(acc_data) without sampling_rate — mandatory positional arg, always raises TypeError
predict_pipeline_acceleration with tz-aware DatetimeIndex — BioPsyKit _resample_index reinterprets nanosecond .asi8 as microseconds, guaranteed OutOfBoundsDatetime overflow
tz_convert('UTC').tz_localize(None) as overflow fix — produces naive UTC DatetimeIndex but .asi8 values are unchanged nanosecond integers; overflow persists identically
content.synthesised_diagnosis: All three failures stem from a single incompatibility: the dataset's DatetimeIndex stores epoch values as nanoseconds (~1.57e18), but BioPsyKit's _resample_index unconditionally calls pd.to_datetime(raw_integers, unit='us'), treating them as microseconds. This overflows pandas' datetime range. Round 1 never reached this code; rounds 2 and 3 both hit it. Round 3's timezone-stripping did not help because tz status does not affect .asi8 magnitude — the fix must rescale the integer values themselves.
content.rationale: BioPsyKit's _resample_index hard-codes unit='us' when converting index integers; nanosecond-resolution pandas DatetimeIndex always overflows this conversion regardless of timezone. No BioPsyKit API parameter controls this behaviour, so the index must be downsampled to microsecond resolution before the call.
content.recommended_alternative: Reconstruct the DatetimeIndex at microsecond resolution before calling predict_pipeline_acceleration: compute epoch_us = (data.index.asi8 // 1000), then data.index = pd.to_datetime(epoch_us, unit='us', utc=True).tz_localize(None). This rescales integers from nanoseconds to microseconds so BioPsyKit's pd.to_datetime(..., unit='us') call stays within bounds. Then infer sampling_rate and call predict_pipeline_acceleration(acc_data, sampling_rate=sampling_rate) as before.
